Dear members of the council, I write concerning the proposed closure of the riverside footpath between Mill Lane and the old wharf. The consultation document describes the path as "lightly used", a phrase that will surprise the several hundred of us who walk it every day.

The path is the only step-free route between the eastern estates and the town centre. Closing it forces wheelchair users and parents with prams onto the Harbour Road pavement, which narrows to eighty centimetres at the bridge and carries lorry traffic to the depot.

The stated reason for closure is the cost of repairing the retaining wall, quoted at ninety thousand pounds. The same report prices the diversion signage, dropped kerbs and crossing upgrades the closure would require at seventy-two thousand pounds, a figure that does not include the resurfacing of Harbour Road that would surely follow.

I ask the council to publish the footfall counts behind the "lightly used" description, to cost a phased repair of the wall alongside the closure option, and to hold the public meeting in the evening rather than at two in the afternoon, when the people who use the path to get to work cannot attend.

There is also the matter of the schoolchildren. Forty-one pupils from the eastern estates walk the path to the primary school each morning, according to the school's own travel survey from last spring. The alternative route adds twelve minutes and two uncontrolled crossings to their journey, and the council's own road safety team recommended against exactly this diversion when it was last proposed in 2019.

Nor should the council forget the traders. The kiosk at the wharf end and the boat-hire office both depend on passing foot traffic for the majority of their summer takings, and both wrote to the consultation in terms that the summary document compresses, with some violence, into the phrase "mixed responses from local businesses".

Yours faithfully, a resident of Mill Lane who would rather keep walking to work than write letters like this one.
